title: SVC Obstruction
oneLiner: Compression or occlusion of the SVC causes facial/upper trunk venous congestion, often from lung cancer; a vascular emergency needing rapid decompression.

sections.Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Obstruction:
**One-liner:** Compression or occlusion of the SVC causes facial/upper trunk venous congestion, often from lung cancer; a vascular emergency needing rapid decompression.

---
sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old male with a 40-pack-year smoking history and recent weight loss presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of progressive facial puffiness, “dizziness” when bending forward, and a feeling of tightness in the neck. On examination, his face is plethoric, jugular veins are distended up to the angle of the jaw, and there is visible collateral veins over his upper chest. Chest x-ray shows a widened mediastinum. He is slightly breathless at rest.

**Dissection of the vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|---|---|
| Age 62, heavy smoker, weight loss | Strong risk for lung cancer (especially small-cell or squamous) – most common cause of SVC obstruction |
| Progressive facial puffing (2 weeks) | Gradual compression of SVC by an extrinsic mass; collateral circulation is developing but insufficient |
| "Dizziness when bending forward" | Reduced preload → cerebral hypoperfusion on positional changes; due to impaired venous return through the SVC |
| Plethoric face (ruddy, suffused) | Venous congestion of the face and upper body; classic sign |
| JVD to jaw angle | Elevated central venous pressure >20 cm H₂O – direct measure of SVC pressure |
| Collateral veins over upper chest | Engorged azygous, internal thoracic, and lateral thoracic veins trying to bypass the block |
| Breathless at rest | May indicate laryngeal oedema or early tracheal compression – a pre‑emergency sign |
| Widened mediastinum on CXR | Suggests a mediastinal mass (e.g., lymphoma, thymoma, or lung cancer) compressing the SVC |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- *Acute onset in a young female* – think central venous catheter-related thrombosis (PICC line, dialysis catheter) causing rapid SVC thrombosis; may mimic pulmonary embolism.
- *Progressive dysphagia and hoarseness* – a patient with known lung cancer who now has SVC syndrome plus recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (left vocal cord paralysis) – indicates extensive mediastinal invasion.
- *“Idiopathic” facial swelling in a fit athlete* – rarely, SVC obstruction can be due to fibrosing mediastinitis from histoplasmosis (endemic area); presents as chronic, painless upper body congestion without mass on imaging.

---
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
SVC obstruction is a partial or complete blockage of blood flow through the superior vena cava back to the right atrium. Because the SVC drains the head, neck, arms, and upper chest, obstruction causes venous congestion upstream – leading to facial swelling, headache, dilated neck veins, and potential airway compromise.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Common in thoracic oncology – about 4% of lung cancer patients develop SVC syndrome (most are small-cell or squamous cell). Also seen in lymphoma, thymoma, and as a complication of central lines/pacemaker wires.
- **Stakes:** It is **not** a slow emergency – rapid progression can cause laryngeal oedema, tracheal obstruction, raised intracranial pressure, and brain herniation. Delay in diagnosis or wrong first step (e.g., giving diuretics) can worsen outcome.
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Confusing with heart failure (HF usually has pedal oedema, crackles, and normal upper body collaterals)  
  - Using diuretics (they reduce preload and may precipitate hypotension in a patient already dependent on high pressure to maintain cardiac output)  
  - Delaying tissue diagnosis when the cause is unclear – SVC obstruction can be the first presentation of a curable malignancy (e.g., lymphoma)

---
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
**The story of how SVC obstruction develops:**

- The SVC is a thin-walled, low-pressure vein lying in the right middle mediastinum. It is surrounded by lymph nodes, trachea, right main bronchus, and the right lung hilum.  
- Anything that grows or clots in this tight compartment can compress or occlude the vessel.  
- **Extrinsic compression** (most common) – a tumour (lung cancer, lymphoma, thyroid mass) presses on the SVC from outside, gradually narrowing the lumen.  
- **Intrinsic thrombosis** – cancer can cause a hypercoagulable state (via tissue factor, mucin), or a foreign body (central line, pacemaker) can trigger a clot inside the vessel.  
- **Fibrosis** – chronic granulomatous disease (histoplasmosis, TB) or post-radiation fibrosis can encase the SVC in a rigid scar.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Facial/neck oedema** | Elevated venous pressure upstream of the block → fluid transudation into soft tissues |
| **Plethora (red face)** | Venous engorgement of the capillaries in the face and conjunctivae |
| **Jugular vein distention** | High SVC pressure transmitted directly to the internal jugular vein (normally 8–12 cm H₂O; here often >20 cm H₂O) |
| **Collateral veins over chest** | The blocked SVC forces blood through the azygous, internal thoracic, and lateral thoracic systems into the inferior vena cava – these dilatations are visible as tortuous veins |
| **Headache and “dizziness”** | Raised intracranial venous pressure reduces cerebral perfusion, especially on bending forward (further compressing the SVC) |
| **Dyspnoea** | May be due to tracheal compression by the same mass, or laryngeal oedema from venous congestion |
| **Hoarseness / Horner’s** | If the mass also invades the recurrent laryngeal nerve or sympathetic chain – indicates more extensive mediastinal involvement |

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern-recognition cues:**  
The patient’s “dizziness when bending forward” is not just a symptom of anaemia or hypotension – it is a positional flow problem. Bending forward increases intra-thoracic pressure, which further compresses the SVC, dropping cardiac output and causing near-syncope. This is a high-yield clue in exams (the “bending forward” sign).

---
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
**Group 1: Malignant – Most common (≈90%)**  
*Cause* | *Stem Clue*  
-- | --  
**Lung cancer (small-cell > squamous)** | Heavy smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis, Pancoast syndrome  
**Lymphoma (NHL > Hodgkin)** | Young adult, painless neck nodes, night sweats, B symptoms  
**Thymoma / thymic carcinoma** | Anterior mediastinal mass on CT, ± myasthenia gravis  
**Metastases (breast, testis, melanoma)** | Known primary, multi-organ involvement  

**Group 2: Benign – Less common (≈10%)**  
*Cause* | *Stem Clue*  
-- | --  
**Central venous catheter thrombosis** | History of PICC, dialysis line, pacemaker; acute onset after line insertion  
**Infectious:** histoplasmosis, TB, actinomycosis | Endemic area, chronic cough, granulomatous mediastinitis  
**Autoimmune:** Behçet’s disease, sarcoidosis | Oral/genital ulcers (Behçet), bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoid)  
**Mediastinal fibrosis (idiopathic or post-radiation)** | Prior chest radiotherapy, slowly progressive over years  
**Aortic aneurysm** | Hoarseness, pulsatile mass on CXR, thoracic aortic dilation  

**Most common cause:** *Small-cell lung cancer* – because it tends to grow centrally in the mediastinum and rapidly compresses the SVC.

---
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria:** There is no formal scoring system for SVC obstruction; diagnosis is clinical + imaging confirmation. The **New York Heart Association (NYHA) Functional Class** is sometimes used to grade severity of dyspnoea, but not as a diagnostic tool.

**Essential tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chest X-ray (PA & lateral)** | Widened mediastinum (right paramediastinal mass, loss of azygous shadow); ± pleural effusion | Quick first-line – may show cause (mass, line, pacemaker) | A normal CXR does **not** rule out SVC obstruction (especially if thrombotic) |
| **CT chest with contrast** | Shows the exact location and length of SVC narrowing; identifies mass, collateral veins, and any intraluminal thrombus | Gold standard for diagnosis and treatment planning | **Single finding that confirms SVC obstruction:** non‑opacification of the SVC with prominent collateral vessels (azygous, internal thoracic) on CT |
| **Venography (digital subtraction)** | Direct visualisation of SVC occlusion | Rarely needed – reserved for planning endovascular stenting | Shows flow obstruction and collaterals; can be used for pressure measurement |
| **Doppler ultrasound of neck veins** | Shows damped, non‑phasic flow in internal jugular and subclavian veins, with no flow variation with respiration | Bedside screening – especially in patients with catheters | **No respiratory variation in jugular vein flow** is highly suggestive of SVC obstruction |
| **Blood tests (FBC, LDH, tumour markers, ESR/CRP)** | Anaemia, raised LDH (lymphoma), ↑β‑hCG (germ cell), ↑ESR/CRP (infection/inflammation) | To identify underlying cause and guide biopsy | **Pattern clue:** LYMPHOMA → very high LDH, normal calcium; LUNG CA → may have hyponatraemia (SIADH) or hypercalcaemia |
| **Biopsy (CT‑guided, mediastinoscopy, or EBUS‑TBNA)** | Histology – is it carcinoma, lymphoma, sarcoid? | Essential to plan definitive treatment | **Important:** Do **not** perform percutaneous trans‑sternal biopsy through the mass (risk of bleeding into mediastinum) |

**What single finding confirms the diagnosis?**  
*CT chest with contrast showing complete loss of contrast in the SVC and a network of collateral veins* – that’s the “gold standard” pattern.

**What definitively rules it out?**  
*Normal CXR *plus* no JVD or facial oedema* makes SVC obstruction very unlikely, but a contrast CT is the only way to exclude it with certainty in a symptomatic patient.

---
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
Management depends on **severity** and **underlying aetiology**. Always treat the emergency first, then the cause.

#### Immediate (first hour) — Airway, Breathing, Circulation, and Steroids

| Action | Dose / Route | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Sit patient upright (45–90°) | – | Reduces venous pressure in the head and neck; improves ventilation |
| High‑flow oxygen (15 L/min via non‑rebreather) | – | Maintains cerebral oxygen delivery and relieves distress |
| **Dexamethasone** | 8 mg IV bolus → then 4 mg q6h | Reduces peritumoural oedema (if extrinsic compression) and laryngeal oedema – buy time for biopsy |
| Check for stridor, use of accessory muscles | – | If stridor present → prepare for urgent bronchoscopy ± intubation (can be very difficult due to laryngeal oedema) |
| **Do NOT give diuretics** | – | They drop preload → worsens hypotension and cerebral hypoperfusion in a patient whose cardiac output may be dependent on high venous pressure |

*If the patient is stable (no stridor, no confusion), skip immediate stenting and focus on diagnosis.*

#### Ward (24–72 hours) — Targeted Therapy

**If malignant cause (most common):**

- **Small cell lung cancer:** Urgent chemotherapy (cisplatin + etoposide) – often works within 48–72 hours to shrink the mass.
- **Non‑small cell lung cancer or lymphoma:** Radiation therapy (30 Gy in 10 fractions) or stereotactic radiotherapy to the mediastinum.
- **Lymphoma:** R‑CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone) – can start within 24 hours after biopsy.
- **Endovascular stenting** – if symptoms are severe and the patient cannot wait for chemo/radiation to work. A self‑expanding metallic stent is placed via the femoral or internal jugular vein. Works immediately to relieve symptoms.

**If thrombotic cause (line‑related):**

- **Remove the line** (if no longer needed) – but beware of embolisation; anticoagulate first if possible.
- **Anticoagulation:** Unfractionated heparin (IV bolus 80 U/kg then 18 U/kg/h) OR low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC bd). Continue until resolution of thrombus or 3–6 months.
- **Catheter‑directed thrombolysis** (e.g., alteplase) – reserved for acute, severe, symptomatic thrombus in young patients with a fresh line.

**What NOT to do in the first 72 hours:**

- ❌ Don’t give prophylactic anticoagulation without a confirmed thrombus (risk of bleeding into the tumour).
- ❌ Don’t start diuretics – they are ineffective and dangerous.
- ❌ Don’t perform a biopsy through the clavicle or sternum – risk of haemorrhage into the mediastinum.
- ❌ Don’t delay tissue diagnosis if the patient is stable – stenting without knowing the cause leads to losing the chance of cure (e.g., lymphoma).

#### Long‑term — Definitive Treatment

| Cause | Long‑term Management |
|---|---|
| Small cell lung cancer | Chemo + prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) |
| Non‑small cell | ChemoRT, targeted therapy (if EGFR/ALK), or surgery |
| Lymphoma | R‑CHOP ± radiation |
| Benign thrombosis | Warfarin or DOAC for 3–6 months; line removal |
| Fibrosing mediastinitis | Steroids, sometimes balloon angioplasty or stenting |

---
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | SVC Obstruction | Congestive Heart Failure | Angioedema | Superior Vena Cava Syndrome-like from Pericardial Effusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pain** | None (pressure sensation only) | Chest pressure if ischaemic | Swelling, itching, often lips/tongue | Chest pain, positional (worse supine) |
| **Exam** | Plethoric face, JVD, collaterals chest | JVD + peripheral oedema, crackles, S3 | Laryngeal oedema, urticaria, no collaterals | Tamponade: pulsus paradoxus, distant heart sounds |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless tumour invasion or PE) | LVH, ST‑T abnormalities | Normal | Low voltage, electrical alternans (big heart) |
| **Labs** | May have ↑ LDH, tumour markers | BNP >> 100 pg/mL | C1‑esterase inhibitor (low levels in hereditary) | Inflammation markers, maybe autoimmune serology |
| **Key differentiator** | Collateral veins over upper chest + CT shows SVC block | Crackles + BNP > 500 + pedal oedema | Rapid onset (minutes‑hours), normal JVP, no collaterals | Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg + echo shows pericardial fluid |

---
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
| Complication | Presentation & Key Signs | What To Do |
|---|---|---|
| **Laryngeal oedema** | Stridor, hoarseness, drooling, respiratory distress | Immediate intubation (difficult – get senior anaesthetist); give IV dexamethasone 8 mg; consider CPAP |
| **Tracheal compression** | Severe dyspnoea, wheeze, use of accessory muscles, obesity may worsen | CT scan, urgent stenting or radiation; avoid bronchoscopy if possible (can cause airway collapse) |
| **Raised intracranial pressure** | Headache, nausea, vomiting, papilloedema, altered GCS | Sit upright, dexamethasone, CT brain to rule out other causes; urgent SVC decompression |
| **Cerebral venous thrombosis** | Seizure, focal deficits, headache out of proportion | MRI venogram; anticoagulate with heparin; manage underlying cause |
| **Superior vena cava rupture** | Sudden chest pain, haemoptysis, shock (rare – can occur during stenting) | Emergency thoracotomy, covered stent, resuscitation |

**Late‑presentation pattern (exam favourite):**  
*A 70‑year‑old male with known lung cancer presents with acute confusion, neck stiffness, and a right‑sided Horner’s. He has facial oedema and JVD. What is happening?* → **Bilateral cerebral venous thrombosis** secondary to SVC obstruction – increased venous pressure leads to thrombosis of the superior sagittal sinus. This is a neurosurgical emergency.

---
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: SVC - Signs & Causes**

| S | **S**welling (facial, neck, arms) |
|---|---|
| V | **V**enous distention (JVD, chest collaterals) |
| C | **C**auses – Cancer (lung, lymphoma), Catheter, Fibrosis |

**Mnemonic for immediate management: D.O.C. - Sit Up, Oxygen, Dexamethasone**

| D | **D**examethasone 8 mg IV |
|---|---|
| O | **O**xygen (high flow) |
| C | **C**ompensatory posturing – sit UPRIGHT |

**5 Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “Pemberton’s sign”** – ask the patient to raise both arms above their head. If facial congestion worsens and they become dizzy, the test is positive. This is a classic exam pearl for SVC obstruction.
2. **Thrombophilic cause** – in a young patient with no smoking history, always think of an indwelling central line or pacemaker wire. Asking “Do you have any lines in place?” is key.
3. **Do not panic about haemoptysis** – it is more likely from the tumour than from the SVC itself. Still, send a CT chest before any procedure.
4. **The “diminished breath sounds on one side”** – indicates pleural effusion; may be the presenting sign of lung cancer causing SVC obstruction.
5. **Stridor + facial puffing = IMCRITICAL** – call ICU immediately; this is a pre‑intubation emergency.

**Common Ward/Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ❌ Giving furosemide for facial oedema – you will worsen preload and cause hypotension.
- ❌ Ordering a D‑dimer – it will be high because of cancer; it does not help distinguish thrombotic from malignant SVC obstruction.
- ❌ Performing a mediastinoscopy without CT – you risk hitting the innominate artery or SVC itself if you are not sure of the anatomy.
- ❌ Assuming SVC obstruction is always a malignancy – do not forget benign causes in a young non‑smoker (histoplasmosis, Behçet’s).

---
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**Call your senior (SpR/Registrar) if:**

- Any stridor, use of accessory muscles, or respiratory rate >30
- GCS <15, confusion, new focal neurology
- Systolic BP <90 mmHg or sustained tachycardia >120 bpm
- SpO₂ <92% on 15 L O₂
- Any suggestion of airway compromise (drooling, unable to speak full sentences)
- NEWS2 score ≥7 (or any single red parameter: RR >24, HR >130, SBP <90)

**Call the crash team / ICU if:**

- **Stridor at rest** – you cannot wait for the registrar; this is a potential airway emergency.
- **GCS ≤8** (coma) – think cerebral venous thrombosis.
- **Cardiac arrest** – SVC obstruction can precipitate PEA arrest from tamponade or massive PE.
- **Seizure** – suspect cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; needs immediate neurology/neurosurgery.
- **NEWS2 ≥9** and escalating.

**Specific thresholds to remember:**

- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (reflecting poor tissue perfusion from low cardiac output)
- **Pulsus paradoxus >15 mmHg** (suspect pericardial effusion)
- **PaCO₂ >45 mmHg** on ABG (impending respiratory failure)
- **Platelets <50 ×10⁹/L** (risk of bleeding if stenting is planned)

rawMarkdown:
## Superior Vena Cava (SVC) Obstruction

**One-liner:** Compression or occlusion of the SVC causes facial/upper trunk venous congestion, often from lung cancer; a vascular emergency needing rapid decompression.

---

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:**
A 62-year-old male with a 40-pack-year smoking history and recent weight loss presents to the Emergency Department with a two-week history of progressive facial puffiness, “dizziness” when bending forward, and a feeling of tightness in the neck. On examination, his face is plethoric, jugular veins are distended up to the angle of the jaw, and there is visible collateral veins over his upper chest. Chest x-ray shows a widened mediastinum. He is slightly breathless at rest.

**Dissection of the vignette:**

| Pattern Clue | What It Tells You |
|---|---|
| Age 62, heavy smoker, weight loss | Strong risk for lung cancer (especially small-cell or squamous) – most common cause of SVC obstruction |
| Progressive facial puffing (2 weeks) | Gradual compression of SVC by an extrinsic mass; collateral circulation is developing but insufficient |
| "Dizziness when bending forward" | Reduced preload → cerebral hypoperfusion on positional changes; due to impaired venous return through the SVC |
| Plethoric face (ruddy, suffused) | Venous congestion of the face and upper body; classic sign |
| JVD to jaw angle | Elevated central venous pressure >20 cm H₂O – direct measure of SVC pressure |
| Collateral veins over upper chest | Engorged azygous, internal thoracic, and lateral thoracic veins trying to bypass the block |
| Breathless at rest | May indicate laryngeal oedema or early tracheal compression – a pre‑emergency sign |
| Widened mediastinum on CXR | Suggests a mediastinal mass (e.g., lymphoma, thymoma, or lung cancer) compressing the SVC |

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**

- *Acute onset in a young female* – think central venous catheter-related thrombosis (PICC line, dialysis catheter) causing rapid SVC thrombosis; may mimic pulmonary embolism.
- *Progressive dysphagia and hoarseness* – a patient with known lung cancer who now has SVC syndrome plus recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy (left vocal cord paralysis) – indicates extensive mediastinal invasion.
- *“Idiopathic” facial swelling in a fit athlete* – rarely, SVC obstruction can be due to fibrosing mediastinitis from histoplasmosis (endemic area); presents as chronic, painless upper body congestion without mass on imaging.

---

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
SVC obstruction is a partial or complete blockage of blood flow through the superior vena cava back to the right atrium. Because the SVC drains the head, neck, arms, and upper chest, obstruction causes venous congestion upstream – leading to facial swelling, headache, dilated neck veins, and potential airway compromise.

**Why it matters clinically:**

- **Frequency:** Common in thoracic oncology – about 4% of lung cancer patients develop SVC syndrome (most are small-cell or squamous cell). Also seen in lymphoma, thymoma, and as a complication of central lines/pacemaker wires.
- **Stakes:** It is **not** a slow emergency – rapid progression can cause laryngeal oedema, tracheal obstruction, raised intracranial pressure, and brain herniation. Delay in diagnosis or wrong first step (e.g., giving diuretics) can worsen outcome.
- **Common pitfalls:**  
  - Confusing with heart failure (HF usually has pedal oedema, crackles, and normal upper body collaterals)  
  - Using diuretics (they reduce preload and may precipitate hypotension in a patient already dependent on high pressure to maintain cardiac output)  
  - Delaying tissue diagnosis when the cause is unclear – SVC obstruction can be the first presentation of a curable malignancy (e.g., lymphoma)

---

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

**The story of how SVC obstruction develops:**

- The SVC is a thin-walled, low-pressure vein lying in the right middle mediastinum. It is surrounded by lymph nodes, trachea, right main bronchus, and the right lung hilum.  
- Anything that grows or clots in this tight compartment can compress or occlude the vessel.  
- **Extrinsic compression** (most common) – a tumour (lung cancer, lymphoma, thyroid mass) presses on the SVC from outside, gradually narrowing the lumen.  
- **Intrinsic thrombosis** – cancer can cause a hypercoagulable state (via tissue factor, mucin), or a foreign body (central line, pacemaker) can trigger a clot inside the vessel.  
- **Fibrosis** – chronic granulomatous disease (histoplasmosis, TB) or post-radiation fibrosis can encase the SVC in a rigid scar.

**Why each key sign happens:**

| Clinical Sign | Pathophysiological Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Facial/neck oedema** | Elevated venous pressure upstream of the block → fluid transudation into soft tissues |
| **Plethora (red face)** | Venous engorgement of the capillaries in the face and conjunctivae |
| **Jugular vein distention** | High SVC pressure transmitted directly to the internal jugular vein (normally 8–12 cm H₂O; here often >20 cm H₂O) |
| **Collateral veins over chest** | The blocked SVC forces blood through the azygous, internal thoracic, and lateral thoracic systems into the inferior vena cava – these dilatations are visible as tortuous veins |
| **Headache and “dizziness”** | Raised intracranial venous pressure reduces cerebral perfusion, especially on bending forward (further compressing the SVC) |
| **Dyspnoea** | May be due to tracheal compression by the same mass, or laryngeal oedema from venous congestion |
| **Hoarseness / Horner’s** | If the mass also invades the recurrent laryngeal nerve or sympathetic chain – indicates more extensive mediastinal involvement |

**Connecting pathophysiology to pattern-recognition cues:**  
The patient’s “dizziness when bending forward” is not just a symptom of anaemia or hypotension – it is a positional flow problem. Bending forward increases intra-thoracic pressure, which further compresses the SVC, dropping cardiac output and causing near-syncope. This is a high-yield clue in exams (the “bending forward” sign).

---

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

**Group 1: Malignant – Most common (≈90%)**  
*Cause* | *Stem Clue*  
-- | --  
**Lung cancer (small-cell > squamous)** | Heavy smoker, weight loss, haemoptysis, Pancoast syndrome  
**Lymphoma (NHL > Hodgkin)** | Young adult, painless neck nodes, night sweats, B symptoms  
**Thymoma / thymic carcinoma** | Anterior mediastinal mass on CT, ± myasthenia gravis  
**Metastases (breast, testis, melanoma)** | Known primary, multi-organ involvement  

**Group 2: Benign – Less common (≈10%)**  
*Cause* | *Stem Clue*  
-- | --  
**Central venous catheter thrombosis** | History of PICC, dialysis line, pacemaker; acute onset after line insertion  
**Infectious:** histoplasmosis, TB, actinomycosis | Endemic area, chronic cough, granulomatous mediastinitis  
**Autoimmune:** Behçet’s disease, sarcoidosis | Oral/genital ulcers (Behçet), bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (sarcoid)  
**Mediastinal fibrosis (idiopathic or post-radiation)** | Prior chest radiotherapy, slowly progressive over years  
**Aortic aneurysm** | Hoarseness, pulsatile mass on CXR, thoracic aortic dilation

**Diagnostic criteria:** There is no formal scoring system for SVC obstruction; diagnosis is clinical + imaging confirmation. The **New York Heart Association (NYHA) Functional Class** is sometimes used to grade severity of dyspnoea, but not as a diagnostic tool.

**Essential tests:**

| Test | What It Shows | Why Order It | Interpretation Pattern |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chest X-ray (PA & lateral)** | Widened mediastinum (right paramediastinal mass, loss of azygous shadow); ± pleural effusion | Quick first-line – may show cause (mass, line, pacemaker) | A normal CXR does **not** rule out SVC obstruction (especially if thrombotic) |
| **CT chest with contrast** | Shows the exact location and length of SVC narrowing; identifies mass, collateral veins, and any intraluminal thrombus | Gold standard for diagnosis and treatment planning | **Single finding that confirms SVC obstruction:** non‑opacification of the SVC with prominent collateral vessels (azygous, internal thoracic) on CT |
| **Venography (digital subtraction)** | Direct visualisation of SVC occlusion | Rarely needed – reserved for planning endovascular stenting | Shows flow obstruction and collaterals; can be used for pressure measurement |
| **Doppler ultrasound of neck veins** | Shows damped, non‑phasic flow in internal jugular and subclavian veins, with no flow variation with respiration | Bedside screening – especially in patients with catheters | **No respiratory variation in jugular vein flow** is highly suggestive of SVC obstruction |
| **Blood tests (FBC, LDH, tumour markers, ESR/CRP)** | Anaemia, raised LDH (lymphoma), ↑β‑hCG (germ cell), ↑ESR/CRP (infection/inflammation) | To identify underlying cause and guide biopsy | **Pattern clue:** LYMPHOMA → very high LDH, normal calcium; LUNG CA → may have hyponatraemia (SIADH) or hypercalcaemia |
| **Biopsy (CT‑guided, mediastinoscopy, or EBUS‑TBNA)** | Histology – is it carcinoma, lymphoma, sarcoid? | Essential to plan definitive treatment | **Important:** Do **not** perform percutaneous trans‑sternal biopsy through the mass (risk of bleeding into mediastinum) |

**What single finding confirms the diagnosis?**  
*CT chest with contrast showing complete loss of contrast in the SVC and a network of collateral veins* – that’s the “gold standard” pattern.

**What definitively rules it out?**  
*Normal CXR *plus* no JVD or facial oedema* makes SVC obstruction very unlikely, but a contrast CT is the only way to exclude it with certainty in a symptomatic patient.

---

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

Management depends on **severity** and **underlying aetiology**. Always treat the emergency first, then the cause.

#### Immediate (first hour) — Airway, Breathing, Circulation, and Steroids

| Action | Dose / Route | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Sit patient upright (45–90°) | – | Reduces venous pressure in the head and neck; improves ventilation |
| High‑flow oxygen (15 L/min via non‑rebreather) | – | Maintains cerebral oxygen delivery and relieves distress |
| **Dexamethasone** | 8 mg IV bolus → then 4 mg q6h | Reduces peritumoural oedema (if extrinsic compression) and laryngeal oedema – buy time for biopsy |
| Check for stridor, use of accessory muscles | – | If stridor present → prepare for urgent bronchoscopy ± intubation (can be very difficult due to laryngeal oedema) |
| **Do NOT give diuretics** | – | They drop preload → worsens hypotension and cerebral hypoperfusion in a patient whose cardiac output may be dependent on high venous pressure |

*If the patient is stable (no stridor, no confusion), skip immediate stenting and focus on diagnosis.*

#### Ward (24–72 hours) — Targeted Therapy

**If malignant cause (most common):**

- **Small cell lung cancer:** Urgent chemotherapy (cisplatin + etoposide) – often works within 48–72 hours to shrink the mass.
- **Non‑small cell lung cancer or lymphoma:** Radiation therapy (30 Gy in 10 fractions) or stereotactic radiotherapy to the mediastinum.
- **Lymphoma:** R‑CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisolone) – can start within 24 hours after biopsy.
- **Endovascular stenting** – if symptoms are severe and the patient cannot wait for chemo/radiation to work. A self‑expanding metallic stent is placed via the femoral or internal jugular vein. Works immediately to relieve symptoms.

**If thrombotic cause (line‑related):**

- **Remove the line** (if no longer needed) – but beware of embolisation; anticoagulate first if possible.
- **Anticoagulation:** Unfractionated heparin (IV bolus 80 U/kg then 18 U/kg/h) OR low molecular weight heparin (e.g., enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC bd). Continue until resolution of thrombus or 3–6 months.
- **Catheter‑directed thrombolysis** (e.g., alteplase) – reserved for acute, severe, symptomatic thrombus in young patients with a fresh line.

**What NOT to do in the first 72 hours:**

- ❌ Don’t give prophylactic anticoagulation without a confirmed thrombus (risk of bleeding into the tumour).
- ❌ Don’t start diuretics – they are ineffective and dangerous.
- ❌ Don’t perform a biopsy through the clavicle or sternum – risk of haemorrhage into the mediastinum.
- ❌ Don’t delay tissue diagnosis if the patient is stable – stenting without knowing the cause leads to losing the chance of cure (e.g., lymphoma).

#### Long‑term — Definitive Treatment

| Cause | Long‑term Management |
|---|---|
| Small cell lung cancer | Chemo + prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) |
| Non‑small cell | ChemoRT, targeted therapy (if EGFR/ALK), or surgery |
| Lymphoma | R‑CHOP ± radiation |
| Benign thrombosis | Warfarin or DOAC for 3–6 months; line removal |
| Fibrosing mediastinitis | Steroids, sometimes balloon angioplasty or stenting |

---

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | SVC Obstruction | Congestive Heart Failure | Angioedema | Superior Vena Cava Syndrome-like from Pericardial Effusion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pain** | None (pressure sensation only) | Chest pressure if ischaemic | Swelling, itching, often lips/tongue | Chest pain, positional (worse supine) |
| **Exam** | Plethoric face, JVD, collaterals chest | JVD + peripheral oedema, crackles, S3 | Laryngeal oedema, urticaria, no collaterals | Tamponade: pulsus paradoxus, distant heart sounds |
| **ECG** | Normal (unless tumour invasion or PE) | LVH, ST‑T abnormalities | Normal | Low voltage, electrical alternans (big heart) |
| **Labs** | May have ↑ LDH, tumour markers | BNP >> 100 pg/mL | C1‑esterase inhibitor (low levels in hereditary) | Inflammation markers, maybe autoimmune serology |
| **Key differentiator** | Collateral veins over upper chest + CT shows SVC block | Crackles + BNP > 500 + pedal oedema | Rapid onset (minutes‑hours), normal JVP, no collaterals | Pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg + echo shows pericardial fluid |

---

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

| Complication | Presentation & Key Signs | What To Do |
|---|---|---|
| **Laryngeal oedema** | Stridor, hoarseness, drooling, respiratory distress | Immediate intubation (difficult – get senior anaesthetist); give IV dexamethasone 8 mg; consider CPAP |
| **Tracheal compression** | Severe dyspnoea, wheeze, use of accessory muscles, obesity may worsen | CT scan, urgent stenting or radiation; avoid bronchoscopy if possible (can cause airway collapse) |
| **Raised intracranial pressure** | Headache, nausea, vomiting, papilloedema, altered GCS | Sit upright, dexamethasone, CT brain to rule out other causes; urgent SVC decompression |
| **Cerebral venous thrombosis** | Seizure, focal deficits, headache out of proportion | MRI venogram; anticoagulate with heparin; manage underlying cause |
| **Superior vena cava rupture** | Sudden chest pain, haemoptysis, shock (rare – can occur during stenting) | Emergency thoracotomy, covered stent, resuscitation |

**Late‑presentation pattern (exam favourite):**  
*A 70‑year‑old male with known lung cancer presents with acute confusion, neck stiffness, and a right‑sided Horner’s. He has facial oedema and JVD. What is happening?* → **Bilateral cerebral venous thrombosis** secondary to SVC obstruction – increased venous pressure leads to thrombosis of the superior sagittal sinus. This is a neurosurgical emergency.

---

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: SVC - Signs & Causes**

| S | **S**welling (facial, neck, arms) |
|---|---|
| V | **V**enous distention (JVD, chest collaterals) |
| C | **C**auses – Cancer (lung, lymphoma), Catheter, Fibrosis |

**Mnemonic for immediate management: D.O.C. - Sit Up, Oxygen, Dexamethasone**

| D | **D**examethasone 8 mg IV |
|---|---|
| O | **O**xygen (high flow) |
| C | **C**ompensatory posturing – sit UPRIGHT |

**5 Clinical Pearls:**

1. **The “Pemberton’s sign”** – ask the patient to raise both arms above their head. If facial congestion worsens and they become dizzy, the test is positive. This is a classic exam pearl for SVC obstruction.
2. **Thrombophilic cause** – in a young patient with no smoking history, always think of an indwelling central line or pacemaker wire. Asking “Do you have any lines in place?” is key.
3. **Do not panic about haemoptysis** – it is more likely from the tumour than from the SVC itself. Still, send a CT chest before any procedure.
4. **The “diminished breath sounds on one side”** – indicates pleural effusion; may be the presenting sign of lung cancer causing SVC obstruction.
5. **Stridor + facial puffing = IMCRITICAL** – call ICU immediately; this is a pre‑intubation emergency.

**Common Ward/Exam Mistakes to Avoid:**

- ❌ Giving furosemide for facial oedema – you will worsen preload and cause hypotension.
- ❌ Ordering a D‑dimer – it will be high because of cancer; it does not help distinguish thrombotic from malignant SVC obstruction.
- ❌ Performing a mediastinoscopy without CT – you risk hitting the innominate artery or SVC itself if you are not sure of the anatomy.
- ❌ Assuming SVC obstruction is always a malignancy – do not forget benign causes in a young non‑smoker (histoplasmosis, Behçet’s).

---

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**Call your senior (SpR/Registrar) if:**

- Any stridor, use of accessory muscles, or respiratory rate >30
- GCS <15, confusion, new focal neurology
- Systolic BP <90 mmHg or sustained tachycardia >120 bpm
- SpO₂ <92% on 15 L O₂
- Any suggestion of airway compromise (drooling, unable to speak full sentences)
- NEWS2 score ≥7 (or any single red parameter: RR >24, HR >130, SBP <90)

**Call the crash team / ICU if:**

- **Stridor at rest** – you cannot wait for the registrar; this is a potential airway emergency.
- **GCS ≤8** (coma) – think cerebral venous thrombosis.
- **Cardiac arrest** – SVC obstruction can precipitate PEA arrest from tamponade or massive PE.
- **Seizure** – suspect cerebral venous sinus thrombosis; needs immediate neurology/neurosurgery.
- **NEWS2 ≥9** and escalating.

**Specific thresholds to remember:**

- **Lactate >4 mmol/L** (reflecting poor tissue perfusion from low cardiac output)
- **Pulsus paradoxus >15 mmHg** (suspect pericardial effusion)
- **PaCO₂ >45 mmHg** on ABG (impending respiratory failure)
- **Platelets <50 ×10⁹/L** (risk of bleeding if stenting is planned)
